publications.title: Emergence of immune escape at dominant SARS-CoV-2 killer T cell epitope.
publications.authors: Dolton G, Rius C, Hasan MS, Wall A, Szomolay B, Behiry E, Whalley T, Southgate J, Fuller A, COVID-19 Genomics UK (COG-UK) consortium, Morin T, Topley K, Tan LR, Goulder PJR, Spiller OB, Rizkallah PJ, Jones LC, Connor TR, Sewell AK.
publications.executive_summary: This study examines the CD8+ T cell response to the dominant SARS-CoV-2 Spike269-277 epitope (YLQPRTFLL) presented by HLA-A∗02, the most frequent human leukocyte antigen class I allotype worldwide. The key finding is that the P272L mutation in Spike, which has emerged independently in at least 112 SARS-CoV-2 lineages including variants of concern, completely escapes recognition by the large polyclonal CD8+ T cell response (>175 distinct TCRs) elicited in both convalescent and vaccinated HLA-A∗02+ individuals. This represents a critical immune escape mechanism: a single point mutation at a prevalent T cell epitope restricted by a high-frequency HLA ablates recognition across a diverse TCR repertoire, demonstrating that vaccine-induced immunity focused narrowly on Spike glycoprotein is vulnerable to viral evolution. The findings underscore the risk of T cell escape at immunodominant epitopes when presented by common HLAs, arguing for inclusion of multiple viral proteins in next-generation COVID-19 vaccines and emphasizing the need for continuous surveillance of T cell escape variants.
